title: The Capital of Comfortable Lies
description: The party arrives in Scriptura. The resistance bookshop. The Guild's chart. The Tempo's hunt through city streets.

lines: Scriptura was beautiful. That was the problem.
Clean streets. Window boxes full of flowers at regulation height. Guards who smiled and said good morning and meant both words in a way that allowed no deviation.
The prices in the market were almost too precise. Not round numbers — precise ones. The kind you get from careful calculation rather than tradition.
It was the most oppressive place any of them had ever seen.

lines.speaker: Rogue
lines.text: Everyone is performing. Even the pigeons.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: I like it here.

lines.speaker: Rogue
lines.text: You would.

steps.text: The resistance operated out of a bookshop. Of course it did. Its shelves were overfull in the way that only looks like disorganization.

lines.speaker: Sera
lines.text: The king is enchanted. Not the fairy-tale kind — the administrative kind.

lines.speaker: Sera
lines.text: Mordaine doesn't need magic chains when she has policy. He signs what she writes. He says what she scripts. He's not a prisoner. He's a process.

lines.speaker: Fighter
lines.text: Can it be undone?

lines.speaker: Sera
lines.text: If someone got through to him? If something broke through the performance long enough for him to act on his own? Maybe. That's not a plan. That's a hope.

steps.text: At the Scriptweaver's Guild — co-opted now, its scholars writing edicts instead of research — one man still kept his old work hidden. He spread documents across a back table.

lines.speaker: Scholar
lines.text: I've been studying this chart for weeks. Every significant location in the kingdom. Every notable threat. Routes between them. I thought it was a political map.

lines.speaker: Scholar
lines.text: It isn't. It's a map of your journey. Every place you've been. Every enemy you've fought. Written before you arrived.

lines.speaker: Mage
lines.text: Someone charted the journey of people they hadn't met yet.

lines.speaker: Scholar
lines.text: Yes.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: You're certain this is about political structure? Not something else?

lines.speaker: Scholar
lines.text: I was certain. I am less certain now.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: I thought I had a word for it and then I didn't.

steps.text: The Mage closed his notebook with the precision of someone deciding not to read something aloud. Whatever he had written, he kept it.
They found the Tempo of the Hunt in the market district. More precisely: it found them. It dropped from a rooftop and landed in front of a vegetable stall without disturbing a single turnip.

lines.speaker: Tempo
lines.text: You made it to the city. I had calculated you would lose yourselves in the forest and give up.

lines.speaker: Tempo
lines.text: I even cleared one of the loop paths to give you a chance. I wanted you to last longer.

lines.speaker: Rogue
lines.text: You helped us.

lines.speaker: Tempo
lines.text: I extended the game. Don't let me catch you. That's not a threat. That's the game.

lines: The chase through Scriptura's streets was everything the Tempo had promised.

It vaulted crates. Disappeared around corners. Reappeared somewhere improbable. It narrated as it ran — not to taunt, but with the genuine delight of something that had been doing this since before the city had crowds.
'Clever — you're slowing down the right moments.' 'Oh, they've learned the geometry. Most don't.'

lines.speaker: Mage
lines.text: It's not trying to kill us. It's playing.

lines.speaker: Rogue
lines.text: It's doing both. It's playing because it enjoys it and the data is incidental. That's worse somehow.

steps.text: The Rogue did something that had no name in any training manual. The geometry of it wasn't strictly possible. The Tempo found itself cornered against a wall, surprised for the first time in what might have been a very long time.

lines.speaker: Tempo
lines.text: Oh.

lines.speaker: Tempo
lines.text: Noted.
